Q1: I tried to keep a fairly consistent study routine, dedicating most of my afternoons and evenings to coursework since I'm taking ten online courses. I'd usually block out chunks of time, maybe four to five hours a day, and work through material systematically. Being a visual learner, I spent a lot of time creating diagrams and mind maps rather than just reading through text. Honestly, the routine was there, but my motivation to stick with it fluctuated quite a bit throughout the semester.
Q2: My pattern was somewhat regular, but it definitely shifted when deadlines approached. I'd try to maintain about 34 hours per week of study time, spread across most days. When multiple assignments converged, I'd have to abandon my usual schedule and just focus on whatever was most urgent. The stress of juggling ten courses meant I couldn't always be as structured as I wanted to be.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or seeing if grades had been posted that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch lecture videos or check discussion boards when I remembered I should probably participate. To be honest, I didn't feel a strong pull toward the LMS itself—it was more about obligation than genuine interest. I'd often log in, grab what I needed, and then work offline.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I naturally gravitated toward. I'd check it when I remembered or when I got email notifications about deadlines. The peer support group I was connected with actually reminded me more often to check in than any habit I developed on my own. It always felt like a tool I used rather than a space I inhabited.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn best visually. I could pause them, rewind, and take screenshots of diagrams or important slides to study later. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful too, especially when peers explained concepts in different ways than the instructor. Having that visual content I could control at my own pace made a real difference in my comprehension.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials and supplementary PDFs unless they had good visuals. Long text documents just didn't stick with me the way videos and diagrams did. I also didn't engage much with the optional practice quizzes—they felt like busywork when I was already stretched thin across ten courses. My assignment completion rate probably reflects that I was selective about what I actually invested time in.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand concepts I'd need for exams or major assignments. Visual resources, clear explanations, and anything that would improve my grades made the cut. If something felt like it was just padding the course or wouldn't impact my final performance, I'd deprioritize it. With medium stress levels and low motivation, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required work got done because it had to, but optional stuff fell by the wayside unless I was genuinely curious or it seemed like easy points. There's a mental weight difference too—required activities carried this pressure that optional ones didn't, which sometimes made the optional ones more appealing but less urgent. I rarely got to them.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides, take screenshots of video content, and create my own visual study guides on paper. I have good resources at home, so I'd print things out and spread materials across my desk to see everything at once. My textbooks and handwritten notes were probably more central to my learning than anything I did directly in the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing printed materials or working through problems in a notebook without touching the LMS at all. I'd watch a video once, take notes, and then study those notes repeatedly offline. Group study sessions with peers from the support program also happened outside the system entirely. My exam scores reflect learning that the LMS just couldn't capture.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture video or download a PDF once, then work with that material for days without logging back in. My approach was to gather everything I needed in one session and then retreat to my own study space. The LMS was like a library I'd visit to check out materials, not a place I'd stay and work in.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really difficult. The workload from ten courses piled up simultaneously, and my motivation just tanked. I felt surprised by how overwhelmed I became even though I'd been managing okay before. The peer support helped somewhat, but there were stretches where I was going through the motions without really engaging mentally. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me harder than they probably should.
Q13: Fatigue was a constant companion, honestly. Managing ten online courses meant I was always somewhat exhausted, which affected how deeply I could engage with LMS activities. When I was tired, I'd log in and scroll through content without really absorbing it. Competing responsibilities and the sheer volume of work meant the LMS often got the minimum viable attention rather than genuine engagement.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered, or I'd click through discussion posts without really processing what people were saying. Sometimes I'd open materials with good intentions but find myself just staring at the screen. The low engagement level the system probably recorded actually reflects those hollow interactions pretty accurately.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which explains my 33% completion rate. Even knowing something was important, the combination of low motivation and medium stress created this paralysis where I'd avoid starting things. I'd tell myself I'd get to it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come with its own pressures. It's frustrating because I knew better, but knowing and doing are different things.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to engage with certain parts of the LMS. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift toward whatever seemed most pressing from other courses. When deadlines approached, I'd suddenly become very active—logging in multiple times, checking requirements, submitting work. My engagement was very deadline-driven rather than consistent.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback on my assignments—which scored pretty well at 91.65—gave me a brief boost, but it didn't translate into sustained motivation. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage entirely rather than improve. With my low resilience, feedback felt more emotionally charged than purely informational, which complicated how I responded to it.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my other nine courses, bad because without rigid structure, I'd let things slide. The weekly modules were predictable, which helped me plan, but the lack of mandatory synchronous sessions meant I could always justify putting things off. More structure might have helped someone with my motivation issues.
Q19: The visual resources made engagement easier—video lectures, diagrams, anything I could see and process visually worked well for me. What made it harder was the volume of text-heavy readings and the discussion requirements that felt forced. The peer interaction expectations were manageable but added another thing to track. Having clearer visual roadmaps of what to prioritize would have helped enormously.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious about the course and keeps checking in without actually being able to focus on producing work. They might be reading and re-reading, watching videos multiple times, but the stress prevents them from actually completing assignments. It's like being stuck in preparation mode without ever feeling ready to execute. The activity shows effort, but something's blocking the output.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My exam score was 83 and I got a Distinction despite relatively low LMS engagement. I did most of my meaningful learning offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed slides spread across my desk, creating visual summaries. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing at all.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in. They can track submission times and participation in discussions. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was actually thinking or just clicking. They miss offline study, printed materials, peer conversations, and the mental work that happens away from the screen. It's measuring presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's when your mind is actively wrestling with concepts rather than passively absorbing information. For me, real engagement happened when I was creating visual study aids or explaining something to a peer—moments of genuine cognitive effort. It's internal and hard to measure, which is probably why systems focus on clicks instead.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed materials, creating hand-drawn diagrams, or studying from my own visual notes. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that helped me stay somewhat on track despite low motivation. It also wouldn't show the internal struggle—the stress, the moments of feeling overwhelmed, the effort it took to produce work despite not feeling motivated. The human experience of learning is invisible to these systems.
